title: pleiodb rho matrix examination
section: Clustered heatmap (top 200 traits by |rho| with others)
text:
Clustered heatmap (top 200 traits by |rho| with others)
To keep the heatmap tractable, we select the 200 traits that have the highest mean |rho| with all other traits.


Code
mean_abs_rho = np.nanmean(np.abs(rho), axis=1)
top200_idx = np.argsort(mean_abs_rho)[::-1][:200]
top200_idx_sorted = np.sort(top200_idx)

rho_sub = rho[np.ix_(top200_idx_sorted, top200_idx_sorted)]
labels = traits.iloc[top200_idx_sorted]["trait_name"].str[:40].values

# Replace NaN with 0 for clustering distance
rho_sub_nonan = np.where(np.isnan(rho_sub), 0.0, rho_sub)
np.fill_diagonal(rho_sub_nonan, 1.0)

# Hierarchical clustering on dissimilarity 1 - |rho|
dist = squareform(1.0 - np.abs(rho_sub_nonan), checks=False)
Z = linkage(dist, method="average")
order = leaves_list(Z)

rho_ord = rho_sub_nonan[np.ix_(order, order)]
labels_ord = labels[order]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 12))
cmap = sns.diverging_palette(220, 20, as_cmap=True)
im = ax.imshow(rho_ord, aspect="auto", cmap=cmap, vmin=-1, vmax=1,
               interpolation="nearest")
ax.set_xticks(range(200))
ax.set_yticks(range(200))
ax.set_xticklabels(labels_ord, fontsize=3, rotation=90)
ax.set_yticklabels(labels_ord, fontsize=3)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.6, label="rho")
ax.set_title("Clustered rho heatmap — top 200 traits by mean |rho|")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: EAF (effect allele frequency) replaces RAF
text:
The per-variant allele frequency stored in pleiodb is the frequency of the effect allele (A2 — the alphabetically-second allele), not the reference allele frequency (RAF) that most GWAS tools conventionally report. This follows directly from the ALID convention (ADR-0001): since A2 is always the effect allele, its frequency is the unambiguous quantity for beta/SE reconstruction.


The --raf CLI flag is removed. The variant input file (--variants) is a two-column TSV of ALID | EAF. When ingesting a VCF in non-canonical orientation (genome-ref REF > ALT alphabetically), EAF = 1 − VCF AF. The raf.f16 binary file in the database is renamed to eaf.f16.

title: ALID as the canonical variant identifier
text:
Variants are identified by CHROM:POS_A1_A2 (ALID) where A1 < A2 alphabetically, rather than by rsID or genome-reference-aligned CHROM:POS:REF:ALT. This encoding is reference-genome-free: all coordinates (chromosome, position, both alleles) are parseable from the string alone, and the canonical orientation is determined by alphabetical order of the alleles rather than by which allele matches the genome reference sequence.



rsID — requires annotation lookup; not universally available for all variants.
CHROM:POS:REF:ALT (genome-ref aligned) — requires a reference genome to define REF; orientation varies across GWAS studies and builds.
ALID (alphabetical) — reference-free, unambiguous, computable from any two-allele record regardless of source. Chosen.




When ingesting a GWAS-VCF whose genome-ref REF > ALT alphabetically, the z-score is negated on read. The effect allele frequency (EAF) stored is always the frequency of A2 (the alphabetically-second allele), which is the consistent effect allele across all traits.

title: Compress long alleles in ALIDs; store variants as TSV not binary
text:
variants.npy (structured array with U512 allele fields) and eaf.f16 are replaced by a human-readable variants.tsv inside the .pleiodb directory with columns ALID and EAF. At 1M variants with typical SNP alleles (~1 char each), the numpy U512 dtype allocates 2 048 bytes per allele — ~4.3 GB total — while the TSV is ~30 MB. chrom, pos, a1, a2 are derived by parsing the ALID on load and are not stored separately.



Alleles longer than 20 characters are compressed to {allele[:8]}~{sha256(allele)[:4]} (13 chars) in both the ALID and the variants.tsv. The tilde (~) is the truncation marker — not a valid nucleotide character. Four hex chars of SHA-256 (16 bits) are sufficient for collision resistance because uniqueness is only required among alleles at the same genomic position, where at most a handful of distinct long alleles ever appear.




ALIDs for long-allele variants are not fully reversible to the original sequence without the source VCF.
Any tool that generates ALIDs must apply the same compression rule for alleles > 20 chars.

title: pleiodb reference completion — regional visualisation
text:
Code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../src")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
import scipy.stats
import zstandard, pathlib

from pleiodb.db import GWASDatabase
from pleiodb.quantize import decode_z

db     = GWASDatabase.open("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb")
traits = pd.read_csv("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb/traits.tsv", sep="\t")
v      = db.variants
V, T   = db.V, db.T

v_chrom = np.array([v[i]["chrom"] for i in range(V)])
v_pos   = np.array([v[i]["pos"]   for i in range(V)], dtype=np.int64)
v_id    = np.array([v[i]["id"]    for i in range(V)])

# ---- imputed COO lookup ------------------------------------------------
blob  = zstandard.ZstdDecompressor().decompress(
    (pathlib.Path("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb") / "imputed.coo.zst").read_bytes()
)
pairs    = np.frombuffer(blob, dtype=np.uint32).reshape(-1, 2)
_t_order = np.argsort(pairs[:, 1], kind="stable")
_t_s     = pairs[_t_order, 1]
_v_s     = pairs[_t_order, 0]
_t_bounds = np.searchsorted(_t_s, np.arange(T + 1))

def imp_set(t_idx: int) -> np.ndarray:
    lo, hi = int(_t_bounds[t_idx]), int(_t_bounds[t_idx + 1])
    return _v_s[lo:hi]

# ---- helper: fetch region data -----------------------------------------
WINDOW_BP = 1_000_000

def region_df(trait_id: str, chrom: str, centre_pos: int) -> pd.DataFrame:
    t = int(db.trait_index([trait_id])[0])
    z_all = db.zscore_trait(t)
    imp_v = imp_set(t)
    imp_set_t = set(imp_v.tolist())

    win = np.where(
        (v_chrom == chrom) &
        (v_pos >= centre_pos - WINDOW_BP) &
        (v_pos <= centre_pos + WINDOW_BP)
    )[0]

    z_win = z_all[win]
    pval  = 2 * scipy.stats.norm.sf(np.abs(z_win))
    logp  = -np.log10(np.maximum(pval, 1e-300))
    is_imp = np.array([w in imp_set_t for w in win])

    return pd.DataFrame({
        "pos":     v_pos[win],
        "id":      v_id[win],
        "z":       z_win,
        "logp":    logp,
        "imputed": is_imp,
    })


Reference completion fills in z-scores for pleiodb variants that are absent in a given study. Each panel below shows the same ±1 Mb cis window for a trait with substantial imputation. Blue circles are directly observed (from the study VCF); orange triangles are imputed by the LD elastic-net pass.
The before picture is the blue points only; after is both.


Code
regions = [
    dict(
        trait_id  = "ebi-a-GCST90025958",
        name      = "Sex hormone-binding globulin",
        chrom     = "14",
        centre    = 94006000,
        gene      = "SHBG (chr14:~94 Mb)",
    ),
    dict(
        trait_id  = "ebi-a-GCST90025953",
        name      = "Total cholesterol",
        chrom     = "2",
        centre    = 20449000,
        gene      = "APOB (chr2:~20 Mb)",
    ),
    dict(
        trait_id  = "ebi-a-GCST005063",
        name      = "Glomerular filtration rate",
        chrom     = "1",
        centre    = 163313000,
        gene      = "chr1:~163 Mb",
    ),
]

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(15, 12))
gs  = gridspec.GridSpec(len(regions), 2, figure=fig, wspace=0.25, hspace=0.50,
                        width_ratios=[1, 1])

for row, r in enumerate(regions):
    df = region_df(r["trait_id"], r["chrom"], r["centre"])
    df = df.dropna(subset=["z"])

    obs = df[~df["imputed"]]
    imp = df[ df["imputed"]]

    pos_mb = df["pos"] / 1e6

    # ---- left panel: observed only ("before") ----
    ax_b = fig.add_subplot(gs[row, 0])
    ax_b.scatter(obs["pos"] / 1e6, obs["logp"],
                 s=18, color="steelblue", alpha=0.75, label=f"observed (n={len(obs)})",
                 linewidths=0, zorder=3)
    ax_b.axhline(-np.log10(5e-8), color="red", lw=0.8, ls="--", alpha=0.6)
    ax_b.set_xlim((r["centre"] - WINDOW_BP) / 1e6, (r["centre"] + WINDOW_BP) / 1e6)
    ax_b.set_xlabel("Position (Mb)", fontsize=8)
    ax_b.set_ylabel("−log₁₀ p", fontsize=8)

title: Imputation performance benchmark
text:
We evaluate how well the elastic-net LD imputation recovers masked z-scores in two contrasting genomic settings:

Null region — chromosome 1 block 168414490–169838848, BMI GWAS (ukb-b-19953). No genome-wide significant variants; z-scores are noise.
Signal region — chromosome 16 block 53815773–55761660, the FTO locus for BMI (ukb-b-19953, N≈462k). Strong association signal (min p ≈ 7×10⁻²⁸).

For each block we:

Load the standardised GWAS summary statistics and the LD reference panel.
Match GWAS SNPs to the LD panel by SNP ID.
Randomly mask 30 % of matched variants (set z-scores to NaN).
Impute the masked values using the PCA + elastic-net method implemented in pleiodb.impute.
Compare imputed z-scores to the held-out truth.

def load_block(gwas_path: Path, block_str: str):
    """
    Load GWAS + LD panel for one block, inner-join on SNP ID, return a dict with:
      z_full, eaf, ld_sub, block_name, n_gwas, n_panel, n_matched
    """
    chrom, block_name = block_str.split("/", 1)
    tsv_path = LD_PANEL / chrom / f"{block_name}.tsv"
    ld_gz    = LD_PANEL / chrom / f"{block_name}.unphased.vcor1.gz"

    gwas  = pd.read_csv(gwas_path, sep="\t")
    panel = pd.read_csv(tsv_path, sep="\t")

    # Canonical SNP ID: strip "chr" prefix if present
    gwas["snp_bare"]  = gwas["SNP"].str.lstrip("chr")
    panel["snp_bare"] = panel["SNP"].str.lstrip("chr")

    # Inner join
    merged = gwas.merge(panel.reset_index().rename(columns={"index": "ld_row"}),
                        on="snp_bare", how="inner")

    ld_row_indices = merged["ld_row"].tolist()
    ld_sub = _load_ld_submatrix(ld_gz, ld_row_indices)

    return dict(
        z_full     = merged["Z"].values.astype(np.float64),
        eaf        = merged["EAF_x"].values.astype(np.float64),
        ld_sub     = ld_sub,
        block_name = block_name,
        n_gwas     = len(gwas),
        n_panel    = len(panel),
        n_matched  = len(merged),
    )


def run_benchmark(block_data: dict, mask_frac: float = MASK_FRAC, seed: int = SEED,
                  thresh: float = LD_THRESH):
    """
    Mask mask_frac of z-scores, impute, return results dict.
    """
    z_full = block_data["z_full"]
    ld_sub = block_data["ld_sub"]
    n = len(z_full)

    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    mask_idx = rng.choice(n, size=int(n * mask_frac), replace=False)

    z_masked = z_full.copy()
    z_masked[mask_idx] = np.nan

    # PCA
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = _ld_pca(ld_sub, thresh=thresh)
    n_comp = len(eigenvalues)

    # Elastic net imputation
    z_pred = _elastic_net_impute(z_masked, eigenvectors, n_comp)
    if z_pred is None:
        return None

    # Polynomial rescaling
    z_adj, corr = _poly_rescale(z_masked, z_pred, npoly=3)

    truth    = z_full[mask_idx]
    imputed  = z_adj[mask_idx]

    rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((truth - imputed) ** 2)))
    slope, intercept = np.polyfit(truth, imputed, 1)
    r2 = corr ** 2 if np.isfinite(corr) else float("nan")

    return dict(
        truth     = truth,
        imputed   = imputed,
        z_masked  = z_masked,
        z_adj     = z_adj,
        corr      = corr,
        r2        = r2,
        rmse      = rmse,
        slope     = slope,
        intercept = intercept,
        n_comp    = n_comp,
        n_masked  = len(mask_idx),
        mask_idx  = mask_idx,
    )

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

for ax, res, title in zip(
    axes,
    [null_res, signal_res],
    ["Null region (chr1 168–170 Mb)", "Signal region (FTO, chr16 53.8–55.8 Mb)"]
):
    truth   = res["truth"]
    imputed = res["imputed"]

    # Density scatter
    xy = np.vstack([truth, imputed])
    try:
        from scipy.stats import gaussian_kde
        z = gaussian_kde(xy)(xy)
        idx = z.argsort()
        sc = ax.scatter(truth[idx], imputed[idx], c=z[idx], s=6, cmap="viridis",
                        rasterized=True)
        plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, label="Density")
    except Exception:
        ax.scatter(truth, imputed, s=4, alpha=0.3, color="steelblue")

    # Identity line
    lim = max(abs(truth).max(), abs(imputed).max()) * 1.05
    ax.axline((0, 0), slope=1, color="tomato", linewidth=1.2, linestyle="--", label="y=x")

    # OLS fit line
    x_line = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 200)
    ax.plot(x_line, res["slope"] * x_line + res["intercept"],
            color="orange", linewidth=1.2, label=f"OLS (slope={res['slope']:.2f})")

    ax.set_xlim(-lim, lim)
    ax.set_ylim(-lim, lim)
    ax.set_xlabel("True z-score")
    ax.set_ylabel("Imputed z-score")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    ax.text(0.05, 0.93, f"r = {res['corr']:.3f}\nRMSE = {res['rmse']:.3f}",

Interpretation:

In the null region (no true signal), imputation recovers z-scores with modest correlation. The elastic-net model captures the LD structure well enough to reproduce the background noise pattern, though RMSE is non-trivial because z-scores are small.
In the signal region (FTO locus), imputation is substantially better: the large z-scores at the association peak are reproduced faithfully because nearby LD proxies carry almost the same information. This is exactly the setting where imputation is most valuable for pleiodb — a dense association signal that was not tested in a sparse array-based study.
Correlation degrades gracefully as the mask fraction increases (see Section 7), but remains useful even at 60–70% masking in the signal region.

text:
Build-level imputation: trial100 (hg19 VCFs)
This section examines how many variants are actually imputed in the trial100_imputed_v2 database, which was built from hg19 VCF files using the hg38 LD reference panel.
Important context: The source VCF files are in hg19 coordinates but the LD panel is in hg38. During the build, the variant list is automatically lifted from hg19 → hg38 so that ALIDs can be matched against the LD panel. The original hg19 ALID remains the primary identifier and is what is stored in the z-score matrix; the hg38 ALID is only used for LD block matching and is stored as the alid_hg38 column in variants.tsv.

def load_imputed_coo(db_path, V, T):
    raw = zstd.ZstdDecompressor().decompress((db_path / "imputed.coo.zst").read_bytes())
    n = len(raw) // 8
    if n == 0:
        return np.zeros((V, T), dtype=bool)
    pairs = np.frombuffer(raw, dtype=np.uint32).reshape(n, 2)
    mask = np.zeros((V, T), dtype=bool)
    mask[pairs[:, 0], pairs[:, 1]] = True
    return mask

imp_v2 = load_imputed_coo(DB_V2, V, T)
imp_v1 = load_imputed_coo(DB_V1, V, T) if DB_V1.exists() else None

# Load z-scores for missingness comparison
z_mat = decode_z(db._zscore.get_block(0, V, 0, T))
n_finite = int(np.isfinite(z_mat).sum())
n_total  = V * T
n_missing_before = n_total - (n_finite - int(imp_v2.sum()))  # missing before imputation

print(f"\nImputation statistics (v2 — with hg19→hg38 liftover fix):")
print(f"  Cells imputed:          {imp_v2.sum():,}")

print(f"  Total finite z-scores:  {n_finite:,}  ({100*n_finite/n_total:.1f}% of V×T)")

Database summary

V = 95,378 variants (hg38, canonical A1 < A2 orientation)
T = 4,159 OpenGWAS traits
Storage: 512×512 zstd-compressed int16 chunks, ti-major order
Imputed pairs: 1,953,848 (LD elastic-net, dense VCF mode)
Rho matrix: sample-overlap-weighted CML estimator, same chunk layout

title: pleiodb query benchmark
text:
Code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../src")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.stats
import zstandard, pathlib, time
from pleiodb.db import GWASDatabase
from pleiodb.quantize import decode_neff, reconstruct_beta_se

db = GWASDatabase.open("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb")
traits_meta = pd.read_csv("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb/traits.tsv", sep="\t")

v_chrom   = np.array([v["chrom"] for v in db.variants])
v_pos     = np.array([v["pos"]   for v in db.variants])
v_id_arr  = np.array([v["id"]    for v in db.variants])
eaf_arr   = db.eaf  # float32(V)

# --- Imputation lookup (sorted COO → per-variant index) ---
coo_path = pathlib.Path("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb/imputed.coo.zst")
_blob    = zstandard.ZstdDecompressor().decompress(coo_path.read_bytes())
_pairs   = np.frombuffer(_blob, dtype=np.uint32).reshape(-1, 2)
_order   = np.argsort(_pairs[:, 0], kind="stable")
_v_sorted, _t_sorted = _pairs[_order, 0], _pairs[_order, 1]
_v_bounds = np.searchsorted(_v_sorted, np.arange(db.V + 1))

def imputed_traits(v_idx: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return array of trait indices imputed for this variant."""
    lo, hi = int(_v_bounds[v_idx]), int(_v_bounds[v_idx + 1])
    return _t_sorted[lo:hi].astype(np.int64)

# --- Chromosome offsets for Manhattan plots ---
CHR_LEN = {
    "1":249250621,"2":243199373,"3":198022430,"4":191154276,"5":180915260,
    "6":171115067,"7":159138663,"8":146364022,"9":141213431,"10":135534747,
    "11":135006516,"12":133851895,"13":115169878,"14":107349540,"15":102531392,
    "16":90354753, "17":81195210, "18":78077248, "19":59128983, "20":63025520,
    "21":48129895, "22":51304566,
}
GAP = 5_000_000
cum_offset = {}
off = 0
for ch in [str(i) for i in range(1, 23)]:
    cum_offset[ch] = off
    off += CHR_LEN.get(ch, 0) + GAP

def phewas_df(v_idx: int) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Tidy PheWAS DataFrame for one variant: alid, trait_id, z, beta_norm, se_norm, pval, eaf, n, imputed."""
    v = int(v_idx)
    z    = db.zscore_variant(v)
    neff = decode_neff(db._neff.get_block(v, v + 1, 0, db.T)[0])
    eaf_v = eaf_arr[v]
    beta, se = reconstruct_beta_se(z, neff, np.array([eaf_v], dtype=np.float32))
    pval = 2 * scipy.stats.norm.sf(np.abs(z))
    imp_t = imputed_traits(v)
    is_imputed = np.zeros(db.T, dtype=bool)
    is_imputed[imp_t] = True
    return pd.DataFrame({
        "alid":      v_id_arr[v],
        "trait_id":  traits_meta["trait_id"].values,
        "trait_name":traits_meta["trait_name"].values,
        "z":         z,
        "beta_norm": beta,
        "se_norm":   se,
        "pval":      pval,
        "eaf":       float(eaf_v),
        "n":         neff,
        "imputed":   is_imputed,
    }).dropna(subset=["z"])

def trait_df(t_idx: int) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Tidy genome-wide DataFrame for one trait."""
    t = int(t_idx)
    z    = db.zscore_trait(t)
    neff = decode_neff(db._neff.get_block(0, db.V, t, t + 1)[:, 0])
    beta, se = reconstruct_beta_se(z, neff, eaf_arr)
    pval = 2 * scipy.stats.norm.sf(np.abs(z))
    # Imputation flag for this trait column
    t_mask = _pairs[_order, 1] == t
    imp_v_set = set(_pairs[_order[t_mask], 0].tolist())
    is_imputed = np.array([i in imp_v_set for i in range(db.V)], dtype=bool)
    return pd.DataFrame({
        "alid":      v_id_arr,
        "chrom":     v_chrom,
        "pos":       v_pos,
        "trait_id":  traits_meta.iloc[t]["trait_id"],
        "z":         z,
        "beta_norm": beta,
        "se_norm":   se,
        "pval":      pval,
        "eaf":       eaf_arr,
        "n":         neff,
        "imputed":   is_imputed,
    }).dropna(subset=["z"])
pleiodb stores 95,378 variants × 4,159 traits as a chunk-compressed binary matrix on disk. All queries go directly to disk — no in-memory index, no server. Output columns are uniform: alid · trait_id · z · beta_norm · se_norm · pval · eaf · n · imputed.

text:
5. Beta reliability — 30 random variants PheWAS

title: pleiodb MR control analyses
text:
Code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../src")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
import scipy.stats
import zstandard, pathlib
from pleiodb.db import GWASDatabase
from pleiodb.quantize import decode_neff, reconstruct_beta_se

db = GWASDatabase.open("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb")
traits_meta = pd.read_csv("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb/traits.tsv", sep="\t")

eaf     = db.eaf
v_chrom = np.array([v["chrom"] for v in db.variants])
v_pos   = np.array([v["pos"]   for v in db.variants], dtype=np.int64)
v_id    = np.array([v["id"]    for v in db.variants])

Z_THRESH = float(db._z_to_pval_threshold(5e-8))

# --- Imputation lookup (per-trait set of imputed variant indices) ---
coo_path  = pathlib.Path("/local-scratch/data/pleiodb/main.pleiodb/imputed.coo.zst")
_blob     = zstandard.ZstdDecompressor().decompress(coo_path.read_bytes())
_pairs    = np.frombuffer(_blob, dtype=np.uint32).reshape(-1, 2)
_t_order  = np.argsort(_pairs[:, 1], kind="stable")
_t_s, _v_s = _pairs[_t_order, 1], _pairs[_t_order, 0]
_t_bounds = np.searchsorted(_t_s, np.arange(db.T + 1))

def imp_set(t_idx: int) -> set:
    lo, hi = int(_t_bounds[t_idx]), int(_t_bounds[t_idx + 1])
    return set(_v_s[lo:hi].tolist())

def get_gwas(trait_id: str):
    t = int(db.trait_index([trait_id])[0])
    z    = db.zscore_trait(t)
    neff = decode_neff(db._neff.get_block(0, db.V, t, t + 1)[:, 0])
    beta, se = reconstruct_beta_se(z, neff, eaf)
    return t, z, beta, se

def clump_1mb(indices: np.ndarray, z_arr: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    order = indices[np.argsort(np.abs(z_arr[indices]))[::-1]]
    kept, taken = [], []
    for i in order:
        c, p = v_chrom[i], int(v_pos[i])
        if not any(c == c2 and abs(p - p2) < 1_000_000 for c2, p2 in taken):
            kept.append(int(i)); taken.append((c, p))
    return np.array(kept, dtype=np.int64)

def mr_ivw(bx, by, sy):
    w    = 1.0 / sy ** 2
    beta = np.sum(w * bx * by) / np.sum(w * bx ** 2)
    se   = np.sqrt(1.0 / np.sum(w * bx ** 2))
    pval = 2 * scipy.stats.norm.sf(abs(beta / se))
    return float(beta), float(se), float(pval)

def run_mr(exp_id, out_id, exp_label, out_label, z_cap=100.0):
    exp_t, z_exp, bx_all, sx_all = get_gwas(exp_id)
    out_t, z_out, by_all, sy_all = get_gwas(out_id)

    sig   = np.where(np.abs(z_exp) >= Z_THRESH)[0]
    valid = sig[
        np.isfinite(by_all[sig]) & np.isfinite(bx_all[sig]) &
        (np.abs(z_out[sig]) <= z_cap)
    ]
    cl = clump_1mb(valid, z_exp)

    # flag imputed in either exposure or outcome
    imp_exp = imp_set(exp_t)
    imp_out = imp_set(out_t)
    is_imp  = np.array([v in imp_exp or v in imp_out for v in cl])

    bx = bx_all[cl]; sx = sx_all[cl]
    by = by_all[cl]; sy = sy_all[cl]

    beta, se, pval = mr_ivw(bx, by, sy)

    obs = ~is_imp
    b_obs, s_obs, p_obs = (mr_ivw(bx[obs], by[obs], sy[obs])
                           if obs.sum() > 2 else (np.nan, np.nan, np.nan))
    b_imp, s_imp, p_imp = (mr_ivw(bx[is_imp], by[is_imp], sy[is_imp])
                           if is_imp.sum() > 2 else (np.nan, np.nan, np.nan))

    return dict(
        exp_label=exp_label, out_label=out_label,
        beta=beta, se=se, pval=pval,
        b_obs=b_obs, s_obs=s_obs, p_obs=p_obs,
        b_imp=b_imp, s_imp=s_imp, p_imp=p_imp,
        n_snps=len(cl), n_obs=int(obs.sum()), n_imp=int(is_imp.sum()),
        instruments=pd.DataFrame({
            "alid": v_id[cl], "chrom": v_chrom[cl],
            "beta_exp": bx, "se_exp": sx,
            "beta_out": by, "se_out": sy,
            "imputed": is_imp,
        }),
    )
Three established positive controls, each scatter-plot coloured by imputation status (in exposure or outcome). Instruments: GWS hits (p≤5×10⁻⁸), distance-clumped at 1 Mb. Outcome |z|>100 excluded as imputation artefacts (see QC discussion below).

The near-absence of imputed instruments (0–6 per analysis) is itself informative: top GWAS hits (p≤5×10⁻⁸) are the best-covered variants in large studies and are virtually never imputed. Imputation fills in weaker signals in smaller or sparser studies.

title: pleiodb MR control analyses
text:
Imputed z-score QC
Exploratory checks reveal two classes of problem in imputed values:
1. Near-sentinel artefacts (|z| ≈ 327.67)
The int16 missing-data sentinel is −32768 (decoded: −327.68). A handful of imputed variants have raw values of ±32767 (decoded: ±327.67) — one step from NA. These arise when the elastic-net extrapolates far outside the range of the training z-scores and the prediction saturates the int16 ceiling. They produce enormous (and clearly spurious) betas in downstream analyses.
2. Inflated but sub-threshold z-scores
More subtly, some imputed z-scores are extreme (|z|>10, |z|>20) without hitting the int16 ceiling. Seen in analyses of small traits (N~10k) where the elastic net model is trained on a handful of non-zero observed z-scores and generalises poorly.
Proposed QC strategy








Step
Method
Rationale




Hard cap during imputation
Clamp predicted z to ±z_max_obs[t] — the maximum absolute z observed for that trait
Prevents extrapolation beyond what the study itself supports


Flag near-sentinel cells
Mark any imputed cell with raw int16 > 32700 as suspect
Catches the specific int16 saturation artifact


Imputation quality score
Store per-cell elastic-net cross-validation R²
Lets users filter on imputation confidence


Per-trait z ceiling
Cap at min(z_pred, sqrt(neff_study)) — the theoretical maximum z under full heritability
Self-calibrating per study size



The most practical immediate fix is the first: during the imputation pass, after predicting z, apply:
z_imputed = np.clip(z_imputed, -np.nanmax(np.abs(z_observed[t])),
                               +np.nanmax(np.abs(z_observed[t])))
This costs nothing, requires no new stored data, and eliminates both artefact classes.


Code
# Illustrate the artefact: distribution of all imputed z-scores for a sample of traits
import random
random.seed(0)
sample_traits = random.sample(range(db.T), 100)

all_imp_z = []
for t in sample_traits:
    lo, hi = int(_t_bounds[t]), int(_t_bounds[t + 1])
    if hi == lo:
        continue
    vs = _v_s[lo:hi]
    z_t = db.zscore_trait(t)
    all_imp_z.extend(z_t[vs][np.isfinite(z_t[vs])].tolist())

all_imp_z = np.array(all_imp_z)
n_art = (np.abs(all_imp_z) > 100).sum()
print(f"Sample: {len(all_imp_z):,} imputed z-scores from 100 traits")
print(f"|z| > 100 : {n_art:,}  ({100*n_art/len(all_imp_z):.3f}%)")

title: ADR-0007: Dense VCF imputation via precomputed LD eigenvectors
text:
Accepted



The post-build imputation pass trains an elastic-net model on the z-scores of stored variants that fall within each LD block. The stored variant set covers only ~20–50 of the ~5–10k reference-panel SNPs per block (0.4–1%). Training on this sparse set gives the model too little signal to reliably recover missing values.
The source GWAS-VCF files contain z-scores for every genotyped or imputed variant in the study — often the full LD-panel variant set. Reading those z-scores at build time (before storing only the ~95k canonical positions) and using them as training data was the natural fix, but the data were discarded before the imputation step ran.



After the VCF ingestion loop completes, for each LD block the imputation worker:

Reads z-scores for all variants in the block region from the source GWAS-VCFs via bcftools view -r (with liftover when the VCF uses a different genome build).
Assembles a dense z-score vector of length N (all reference-panel SNPs in the block) using allele-orientation-aware matching.
Loads precomputed block eigenvectors from .ldeig.rds files that ship with the LD reference panel; caches them as .ldeig.npz after the first read.
Trains the elastic net on the dense z-vector and extracts predictions only at the stored (missing) positions.

The traits TSV gains two new optional columns — vcf_path and build — which are stored in the database and supplied back to the imputation pass. When these columns are absent (old databases), the pass falls back to sparse mode using only stored variants.



Each .ldeig.rds file is ~662 MB (full N×N eigenvector matrix, R binary format). Reading it via Rscript subprocess takes 3–5 s. Only K ≈ 169–250 columns are needed (90% variance threshold). On first access the worker extracts and saves those columns as a compressed .ldeig.npz cache (~7 MB); subsequent reads take ~62 ms. The cache write uses an atomic temp-file-then-rename pattern to avoid corruption under concurrent workers.




Imputation quality improves because the model sees the full local LD signal.
First-pass per-block cost increases slightly (VCF region reads via inner ThreadPoolExecutor), but the eigenvector cache amortises the RDS parse cost so overall wall-clock time is comparable: 0.71 s/block dense vs 0.94 s/block sparse (chr1, 5 traits, 32 ProcessPoolExecutor workers, 8 VCF threads each).
The .ldeig.npz cache files accumulate in the LD reference panel directory (~9 GB for 1345 blocks). This is a one-time cost shared across all builds.
vcf_path / build are optional — existing databases and builds without --ld-dir continue to work unchanged.

title: ADR 0006 — rho: CML estimator replaces lambda/Pearson correlation
text:
Accepted



pleiodb stores a T×T matrix of pairwise trait correlations that captures the combined effect of sample overlap and phenotypic correlation between studies. This quantity is used downstream for z-score correction, multivariate models (SEM, factor analysis), pleiotropy tests (GSMR, MR-RAPS), and conditional F-statistics.
The original implementation (build_lambda) computed this matrix as the Pearson correlation of z-scores across variants with |z| < 3.0 in both traits, and stored the result in lambda.bin/.cidx.
Two problems were identified:

Name collision: “lambda” (λ) standardly denotes the per-trait genomic inflation factor (median(χ²)/0.456) in the GWAS literature. Using the same symbol for a T×T cross-trait matrix creates ambiguity.
Estimator bias: selecting variants on |z| < threshold and then computing Pearson correlation on that selected subset produces a biased estimate. The selection induces a truncated bivariate distribution; the raw Pearson r of truncated data is not an unbiased estimator of the underlying correlation.






The matrix is renamed to rho. The canonical term is “sample-overlap-weighted phenotypic correlation”, abbreviated rho. File names change from lambda.bin/.cidx to rho.bin/.cidx. The CLI command changes from pleiodb lambda to pleiodb rho.



Replace the Pearson estimator with the conditional maximum likelihood estimator from Forde et al. (mr.simss::est_lambda). Given null variants (|z_j| < z_thresh AND |z_k| < z_thresh), the CML estimator maximises:
log L(ρ | data) ∝  −(A − 2ρB + C) / (2(1−ρ²))
                   − n · log P(|X| < z, |Y| < z ; ρ)
where A = Σz_j², B = Σz_j·z_k, C = Σz_k², n = number of null variants, and the denominator is the probability mass of a bivariate normal with correlation ρ falling in the square [−z, z]². This denominator is a function of ρ and z_thresh only (not the data) and is precomputed on a grid to avoid repeated 2D integration.



The default null-variant threshold is set to z_thresh = 1.0. The original code used 3.0 (retaining ~99.7% of variants); the reference implementation uses 0.5 (too aggressive). z = 1.0 retains ~68% of variants per trait and ~46% per pair, giving the CML estimator sufficient data while excluding most true associations. Accuracy requirements for this matrix are moderate (it is an approximation used in downstream corrections, not a primary result).



The rho computation reads from the already-stored z-score matrix rather than re-extracting variants from VCF files. The database variant list is typically enriched for associations (variants known to associate with at least one trait in the matrix), but for any specific pair (j, k) the majority of variants will have low z in both traits, providing an adequate null set.
An alternative (Approach B) — extracting ~1M HapMap3 SNPs independently from VCF files — was considered but rejected as unnecessarily complex given the moderate accuracy requirement.



pleiodb rho remains a separate post-build step (not run automatically by pleiodb build). This allows recomputation with different thresholds without rebuilding the database, and avoids surprising users with a slow step at the end of a long build. pleiodb info warns when the rho matrix is absent.



The pleiodb rho command doubles as a query interface. Presence of --traits (comma-separated trait IDs) or --traits-file (one trait ID per line) puts the command into query mode; absence triggers matrix computation.
Default query output is a pairwise list:
trait_id_1      trait_id_2      rho
ukb-b-19953     ieu-a-7         0.0412
ukb-b-19953     ukb-b-10787     0.1823
ieu-a-7         ukb-b-10787     0.0091
The --matrix flag pivots to a square matrix with trait IDs as row/column headers — convenience for small T and direct input to R/Python matrix methods.
Trait IDs must not contain commas (commas delimit the --traits list). This is already satisfied by all known trait ID conventions (e.g. ieu-a-7).





lambda.bin/.cidx is replaced by rho.bin/.cidx; existing databases built with the old code will not have a rho matrix (they will trigger the pleiodb info warning).
The CML estimator requires scipy.optimize.minimize_scalar and the bivariate normal CDF (scipy.stats.multivariate_normal.cdf); both are already dependencies.
rho[j, j] is set explicitly to 1.0 rather than estimated.
A minimum of 500 null variants is required per pair; pairs falling below this threshold are stored as NaN.
The matrix is enforced to be symmetric: only the upper triangle is computed and mirrored.

title: ADR 0006 — rho: CML estimator replaces lambda/Pearson correlation
text:
pleiodb stores a T×T matrix of pairwise trait correlations that captures the combined effect of sample overlap and phenotypic correlation between studies. This quantity is used downstream for z-score correction, multivariate models (SEM, factor analysis), pleiotropy tests (GSMR, MR-RAPS), and conditional F-statistics.
The original implementation (build_lambda) computed this matrix as the Pearson correlation of z-scores across variants with |z| < 3.0 in both traits, and stored the result in lambda.bin/.cidx.
Two problems were identified:

Name collision: “lambda” (λ) standardly denotes the per-trait genomic inflation factor (median(χ²)/0.456) in the GWAS literature. Using the same symbol for a T×T cross-trait matrix creates ambiguity.
Estimator bias: selecting variants on |z| < threshold and then computing Pearson correlation on that selected subset produces a biased estimate. The selection induces a truncated bivariate distribution; the raw Pearson r of truncated data is not an unbiased estimator of the underlying correlation.

title: ADR 0006 — rho: CML estimator replaces lambda/Pearson correlation
text:
The matrix is renamed to rho. The canonical term is “sample-overlap-weighted phenotypic correlation”, abbreviated rho. File names change from lambda.bin/.cidx to rho.bin/.cidx. The CLI command changes from pleiodb lambda to pleiodb rho.



Replace the Pearson estimator with the conditional maximum likelihood estimator from Forde et al. (mr.simss::est_lambda). Given null variants (|z_j| < z_thresh AND |z_k| < z_thresh), the CML estimator maximises:
log L(ρ | data) ∝  −(A − 2ρB + C) / (2(1−ρ²))
                   − n · log P(|X| < z, |Y| < z ; ρ)
where A = Σz_j², B = Σz_j·z_k, C = Σz_k², n = number of null variants, and the denominator is the probability mass of a bivariate normal with correlation ρ falling in the square [−z, z]². title: bcftools used as the universal VCF pre-filter
text:
Every GWAS-VCF read uses bcftools view -R <positions> to restrict the file to the queried positions before cyvcf2 parses individual records, not only in the liftover path. bcftools is therefore a required runtime dependency (installed via conda/bioconda).



cyvcf2 full scan — simple, no extra dependency. Unacceptably slow for production VCFs with millions of variants; fine only for the small test files.
tabix/cyvcf2 region queries — fast but requires one query call per variant; overhead dominates for 100k variants.
bcftools pre-filter (chosen) — one subprocess call produces a position-restricted VCF; cyvcf2 then does a fast linear scan of the small result. Symmetric behaviour for liftover and non-liftover paths. Falls back to full cyvcf2 scan if no index is present or bcftools is unavailable.

title: Derive Neff from SE and traits.tsv; ignore VCF SS field
text:
Per-variant Neff is computed at ingest as var_y[t] / (SE[v,t]² × 2·EAF[v]·(1−EAF[v])) rather than being read from the VCF SS field. var_y[t] is itself estimated as the median of SE[v,t]² × 2·EAF[v]·(1−EAF[v]) × Neff_study[t] across common variants (EAF ∈ (0.01, 0.99)), where Neff_study[t] = N[t] for continuous traits and 4·N[t]·K[t]·(1−K[t]) for binary traits, with N and K (case fraction) supplied in the traits input file.


The SS FORMAT field is not consistently defined across GWAS software: some tools write total sample size (Ncase + Ncontrol), some write binary effective N (4/(1/Ncase + 1/Ncontrol)), and some omit it entirely. Using raw SS as Neff in the normalised-beta reconstruction formula (SE_norm = 1/sqrt(Neff × 2p(1−p))) requires that SS equals N / var_y — an assumption that holds only incidentally and cannot be verified from the VCF alone.




Read SS from VCF directly — rejected because SS semantics are inconsistent across tools (see above).
Require users to supply Neff explicitly — rejected because Neff for binary traits depends on case fraction in a non-obvious way; N + K is a more natural and auditable input.





ES and SE FORMAT fields are required in every ingested VCF (used to derive both Z and Neff).
N is a required column in the traits input file; K is required for binary traits.
The stored Neff is on the normalised (var(y)=1) scale: it equals the actual study N only when the GWAS formula holds exactly and EAF_user ≈ EAF_study.
The single shared EAF per variant (from the user variants file) introduces a small approximation in Neff and var_y when study-specific EAFs differ from it.
